Styles › Modern

1920s – 1970s · Europe → Global

Modern

Clean lines, functional forms, no ornament.

  • Overview
  • Design Philosophy
  • Visual Characteristics
  • Materials
  • Color Palette
  • Façade Guide
  • Lighting
  • Building Types
  • Design Checklist
  • Common Mistakes
  • AI Prompts
  • Gallery
  • Related

01 — Overview

Overview

Definition. Modernism reduces the building to volume, structure, light and proportion — flat roofs, open façades and an honest expression of concrete, steel and glass, free of historical ornament.

Historical background. Born from industrialisation and post-war reform, Modernism rejected 19th-century revivalism. New materials freed the wall from load, opening the plan and the façade to light and air.

Origin. 1920s Germany, France and the Netherlands; codified as the 'International Style' (MoMA, 1932).

Timeline

1923

Le Corbusier, 'Vers une architecture'

1929

Barcelona Pavilion — 'less is more'

1931

Villa Savoye realises the Five Points

1958

Seagram Building defines the corporate tower

Key Architects

Le CorbusierMies van der RoheWalter GropiusRichard NeutraOscar Niemeyer

Influential Projects

Barcelona Pavilion

Mies van der Rohe · 1929
Barcelona, Spain

Farnsworth House

Mies van der Rohe · 1951
Plano, USA

Seagram Building

Mies van der Rohe · 1958
New York, USA

02 — Design Philosophy

Design Philosophy

Core Principles

Form follows functionTruth to materialsFree plan & free façadeVolume over massNo ornament

The Thinking

The building is a clear diagram of its purpose. Structure is expressed, space flows, and the wall becomes a thin skin. Beauty lives in proportion and the honest assembly of parts.

Values It Represents

HonestyUniversalityEfficiencyLight & air for all

Why It Emerged

A moral and social project — a healthier, more democratic world built with the machine age, replacing aristocratic ornament with universal order.

03 — Visual Characteristics

Visual Characteristics

MassingClean orthogonal volumes, often lifted on slender pilotis so the ground flows beneath; expressive cantilevers.
Form LanguagePure prismatic geometry — cube, slab, cylinder — with no applied decoration.
CompositionAsymmetrical balance on a structural grid; solids and voids weighed like a Mondrian.
ProportionMathematical systems — the Modulor, golden section, repeating bays.
GeometryRectilinear and planar; curves rare and deliberate.
RhythmRegular structural bays produce a steady façade beat.
SymmetryPredominantly asymmetrical — balance by composition, not mirroring.
ScaleHuman in housing, abstract and monumental in civic/corporate work.
Roof TypesFlat roofs, often as accessible terraces or roof gardens.
Window ProportionHorizontal ribbon windows and floor-to-ceiling glazing.
EntranceRecessed, understated, integrated into the volume.
Façade ArticulationThe curtain wall — a taut thin membrane of glass and infill panels expressing the frame behind.

04 — Materials

Materials

Reinforced concrete

Enables the free plan, cantilevers and pilotis.

Structural steel

Slender frames carry load so walls dissolve into glass.

Plate glass

Dissolves the inside/outside boundary; admits light.

White render

A pure abstract skin emphasising volume and shadow.

05 — Color Palette

Color Palette

Crisp, optimistic, luminous — colour as sparing punctuation on a neutral field.

Primary

Architectural White
#f2efe8
Concrete Grey
#b8b3a8

Accent

Primary Red
#b5352b
Cobalt
#2f5e8c
Ochre
#caa14a

Material Colors

Glass Blue
#a8bcc4
Steel
#6f7479

Typical Combinations

  • +White + glass + thin black steel
  • +White render with primary-colour accent planes

06 — Façade Design Guide

Façade Design Guide

Window CompositionContinuous horizontal bands or full-height glazing aligned to the grid; keep mullions thin.
EmphasisHorizontal — read the building as stacked floor planes.
Shading DevicesBrise-soleil concrete fins for sun control — functional, not decorative.
BalconiesCantilevered slabs with thin steel rails as floating horizontal planes.
FramesMinimal, flush, painted dark to recede.
OpeningsLarge, repetitive, grid-aligned; avoid arched or punched single windows.
Architectural DetailsReveals and shadow gaps replace mouldings — the detail is the absence of detail.
OrnamentationNone — interest comes from proportion, material and light.

07 — Lighting

Lighting

Natural Lighting Strategy

Maximise daylight through ribbon and curtain-wall glazing; deep overhangs control glare.

Artificial Lighting Concept

Concealed, even architectural lighting — no decorative fixtures.

Night Façade Appearance

The glass box glows from within, revealing the frame as a luminous grid.

Recommendations

Wash flat surfaces evenly; cool-neutral 3500–4000K; graze concrete to reveal texture.

08 — Typical Building Types

Typical Building Types

Where this style performs best:

VillasOffice towersCultural institutionsHousingUniversitiesMixed-use

09 — Design Checklist

Design Checklist

Tap an item to mark it complete — saved to your browser.

  • Lift the volume on pilotis or express a clear frame
  • Use a flat roof — consider a roof terrace
  • Design a free, open plan
  • Specify ribbon windows or a full curtain wall
  • Eliminate all applied ornament
  • Set a consistent structural grid
  • Detail thin, flush, dark window frames
  • Use colour only as sparse accent

10 — Common Mistakes

Common Mistakes

Elements that contradict the style — what to avoid:

  • ✕Decorative cornices, mouldings or 'classical' details
  • ✕Pitched or busy roofs fighting the flat-volume language
  • ✕Thick, divided muntins breaking the glazing plane
  • ✕Random window placement instead of a grid
  • ✕Busy multi-colour façades

11 — AI Prompt Examples

AI Prompt Examples

Optimised for Midjourney, ChatGPT image tools or Claude — exterior renders. Tap copy to use.

Exterior

Modernist villa exterior, white rendered prismatic volume on slender pilotis, horizontal ribbon windows, flat roof, free façade, lawn, soft daylight, architectural photography, 35mm --ar 3:2 --style raw

Façade Concept

Modernist curtain-wall façade, thin black steel mullions, floor-to-ceiling glass, regular structural grid, white spandrel panels, frontal elevation, clean shadows

Night View

International Style glass tower exterior at dusk, glowing structure, reflective curtain wall, deep blue sky, long exposure, architectural night photography

Aerial View

Aerial of a Modernist campus, orthogonal white slabs on a green podium, flat roofs, geometric clarity, drone photography, morning light

Street Perspective

Street-level Modernist apartment slab, pilotis at ground, ribbon windows above, pedestrian scale, documentary architectural photography

Masterplan

Masterplan of a Modernist district, free-standing slabs in parkland, orthogonal grid, axonometric, muted palette
